steps.title: Where Are You From? 🌍
steps.desc: Dutch people love asking where you're from! In this lesson you'll learn to talk about yourself and your nationality.

steps.note: The most common way to say where you're from. 'Uit' means 'from' here.

steps.exampleEn: Hi! Where do you come from?
steps.note: 'Vandaan' adds emphasis to 'where from'. You can also just say 'Waar kom je?'

steps.exampleEn: The Netherlands is a small country.
steps.note: 'Het land' — a het-word! It also means 'land/countryside'.

steps.title: 🌐 Countries in Dutch
steps.text:
Most country names in Dutch are recognizable:

🇳🇱 Nederland — The Netherlands
🇧🇪 België — Belgium
🇩🇪 Duitsland — Germany
🇫🇷 Frankrijk — France
🇬🇧 Engeland — England
🇺🇸 Amerika — America
🇪🇸 Spanje — Spain
🇮🇹 Italië — Italy
🇹🇷 Turkije — Turkey
🇲🇦 Marokko — Morocco

Notice: country names don't get 'de' or 'het' — they stand alone!

steps.q: How do you say 'I come from Germany'?

steps.note: Use 'Ik ben' + nationality. Note: nationalities are adjectives in Dutch, not nouns!

steps.title: 🏳️ Nationalities
steps.text:
Nationalities in Dutch often end in -s or -e:

🇳🇱 Nederlands — Dutch
🇩🇪 Duits — German
🇫🇷 Frans — French
🇬🇧 Engels — English
🇪🇸 Spaans — Spanish
🇹🇷 Turks — Turkish
🇲🇦 Marokkaans — Moroccan

Examples:
• Ik ben Nederlands. — I am Dutch.
• Zij is Frans. — She is French.
• Hij spreekt Duits. — He speaks German.

The same word is used for the language AND the nationality!

deepDive.title: Nationality = Language in Dutch
deepDive.text:
Unlike English, Dutch uses the same word for nationality and language.

'Hij is Duits' = He is German
'Hij spreekt Duits' = He speaks German

This makes things simpler! The context tells you whether it means the person or the language.

steps.title: I Live In... 🏡
steps.desc: Tell people where you live! This is one of the most common small talk topics in the Netherlands.

steps.note: 'Wonen' specifically means to reside/live somewhere. 'Ik woon' = I live.
A de-word. Plural: de steden (cities). Dutch cities are world-famous!

steps.example: Ik woon in een klein dorp.
steps.exampleEn: I live in a small village.
steps.note: A het-word. The Netherlands has hundreds of charming dorpen (villages).

steps.exampleEn: In which street do you live?
steps.note: A de-word. Many Dutch streets end in '-straat' or '-weg' (road) or '-laan' (lane).

steps.title: 💬 The most common question
steps.text:
In Dutch small talk, 'Waar woon je?' (Where do you live?) is almost as common as 'Hoe gaat het?'

Full pattern:
❓ Waar woon je? — Where do you live?
💬 Ik woon in [city/town]. — I live in [city/town].
💬 Ik woon in de buurt van... — I live near...

Dutch people often follow up with:
❓ Vind je het leuk daar? — Do you like it there?

steps.q: How do you say 'I live in Rotterdam'?

steps.title: How Dutch Verbs Work
steps.text:
Time to conjugate! Here's the pattern:

1. Take the infinitive: wonen
2. Remove -en to get the stem: woon
3. Add endings:

ik → stem (ik woon)
jij → stem + t (jij woont)
hij/zij → stem + t (hij woont)
wij/jullie/zij → infinitive (wij wonen)

This works for almost ALL Dutch verbs!

Full reference → Grammar tab → A1 → How Dutch Verbs Work

steps.title: Languages I Speak 🗣️
steps.desc: The Dutch are famous polyglots! Learn to talk about languages and what you speak.

steps.note: Notice the double 'ee' in the stem — it keeps the long vowel sound. In questions with 'jij': 'Spreek je...?' (no -t!)
Super useful phrase! 'Beetje' literally means 'a bit'. Dutch people will love hearing you try!
A de-word. Plural: de talen. 'Moedertaal' = mother tongue.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 The Dutch and languages
steps.text:
The Dutch are among the best non-native English speakers in the world! Many speak 2-3 languages.

Useful phrases:
• Ik spreek Nederlands. — I speak Dutch.
• Ik spreek een beetje Nederlands. — I speak a little Dutch.
• Spreek je Engels? — Do you speak English?
• Ik leer Nederlands. — I am learning Dutch.
• Ik versta je niet. — I don't understand you.

Pro tip: Even though most Dutch people speak English, they deeply appreciate any effort to speak Dutch!

deepDive.title: Why most Dutch people speak English so well
deepDive.text:
Several reasons make the Dutch such good English speakers:

1. Dutch TV shows and movies are subtitled, not dubbed (unlike Germany or France)
2. English is taught from primary school (age 10-11)
3. Dutch and English are both Germanic languages — they share many words
4. The Netherlands is a small trading nation — English is essential for business

Fun cognates (words that look alike):
• water = water
• boek = book
• appel = apple
• groen = green
• kat = cat

steps.q: How do you say 'I speak a little Dutch'?

steps.title: Conjugation Practice
steps.text:
Remember the pattern from last lesson?

Infinitive: spreken (to speak)
Stem: spreek (remove -en)

ik → spreek
jij → spreekt (stem + t)
hij/zij → spreekt (stem + t)
wij → spreken (= infinitive)

Note: the double 'ee' stays in the stem to keep the long vowel sound. See Grammar → Spelling Rules for Stems.

steps.title: All About Me 💫
steps.desc: Now combine everything! Introduce yourself fully — name, origin, where you live, and what languages you speak.

steps.title: 🎯 Your Dutch self-intro
steps.text:
Here's a full self-introduction pattern:

Hallo! Ik heet [name]. — Hello! My name is [name].
Ik kom uit [country]. — I come from [country].
Ik woon in [city]. — I live in [city].
Ik spreek [language] en een beetje Nederlands. — I speak [language] and a little Dutch.
Aangenaam! — Nice to meet you!

Example:
Hallo! Ik heet Maria. — Hello! My name is Maria.
Ik kom uit Spanje. — I come from Spain.
Ik woon in Amsterdam. — I live in Amsterdam.
Ik spreek Spaans en een beetje Nederlands. — I speak Spanish and a little Dutch.
Aangenaam! — Nice to meet you!

steps.q: Someone says 'Waar woon je?' — you reply:
How do you say 'Nice to meet you' in Dutch?
